attributes.stem: A 16-year-old girl presents to the General Practice clinic concerned as she has "missed the pill". She usually takes the combined oral contraceptive pill each morning but forgot the last two days, meaning that her last active pill was 56 hours ago. Her last withdrawal bleed was three weeks ago. She had unprotected sexual intercourse 48 hours ago. She has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate advice?
options: In view of her age, parental consent is required to prescribe contraception
There is no need to change her pill regimen as contraception is effective until more than four pills are missed
Reassure the patient and strongly recommend long-acting reversible contraceptives
Resume the usual pill cycle from today, regardless of whether active or inactive pills are due
Take the most recently missed pill now, use barrier contraception and continue active hormone pills for at least seven days from today
attributes.explanation:
This patient is in the final week of her pill cycle before taking inactive pills. Missing two pills in this period triggers the '7-day rule'. Taking the most recently missed pill now, using barrier contraception and continuing active hormone pills for at least seven days from today is the most appropriate advice. As she was three weeks into her pill cycle, her contraception was still effective at the time of intercourse, so she does not require emergency contraception in this instance.

The primary mechanism of action of the combined oral contraceptive pill (COCP) is the prevention of ovulation through suppression of the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis, thickening of cervical mucus, and prevention of sperm penetration. Taken correctly, it is 99.7% effective (although typical use efficacy is closer to 91%).

Reassuring the patient and recommending long-acting reversible contraception (LARC) would be inappropriate as additional management is needed. However, it would be appropriate to discuss changing to LARC if this patient continues to, or is likely to, forget her pill in the future. LARC refers to etonogestrel subcutaneous implants and levonorgestrel or copper intrauterine devices (IUD) which have a risk of failure of less than 1%. The copper IUD is also able to be used as emergency contraception if inserted within 120 hours after unprotected intercourse, if this were required.

Contacting the patient's parents or caregivers is not necessary if the patient is a mature minor (also known as Gillick Competent). The age of consent differs between states, and in some states, patients aged 16 years and older can make their own decisions about contraception. If this is not applicable, Gillick competency should be assessed.

Resuming the usual pill cycle without additional precautions would be inappropriate as it does not address the missed pills and potential risk of pregnancy. While contraceptive efficacy may be maintained with fewer missed pills earlier in the cycle, two missed pills in the last week of active pills requires additional precautions.
learningPoints: When two or more combined oral contraceptive pills are missed in the last week of active pills, the '7-day rule' should be applied: continue active pills for 7 days and use barrier contraception.
Emergency contraception is not required if unprotected intercourse occurred within the first 7 days of missing pills, as contraceptive effect is maintained.
Long-acting reversible contraceptives (LARC) should be offered as part of contraceptive counselling, especially for patients who struggle with daily pill compliance.
references: How to take your pill: combined oral contraceptive pill.https://www.familyplanning.org.nz/media/172505/instruction-pad-coc-pill-sept-2014.pdf. Accessed August 28, 2020.

RANZCOG C-Gyn 28 (2019). Combined hormonal contraceptives.https://ranzcog.edu.au/RANZCOG_SITE/media/RANZCOG-MEDIA/Women%27s%20Health/Statement%20and%20guidelines/Clinical%20-%20Gynaecology/Combined-hormonal-contraceptives-(C-Gyn-28)-Review-March-2019.pdf?ext=.pdf. Accessed August 28th, 2020.

RANZCOG C-Gyn 34(2017) Long acting reversible Contraception. https://ranzcog.edu.au/RANZCOG_SITE/media/RANZCOG-MEDIA/Women%27s%20Health/Statement%20and%20guidelines/Clinical%20-%20Gynaecology/Long-acting-reversible-contraception-(C-Gyn-34)-Review-July-2017.pdf?ext=.pdf. Accessed August 28th, 2020.

Shemer, S and Woodrow, N (2016). To treat or not to treat: does Gillick competence answer the question? O&G Magazine ,Vol 18 No 3. Spring 2016. https://www.ogmagazine.org.au/18/3-18/treat-not-treat-gillick-competence-answer-question/. Accessed August 28, 2020.

attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
